- CAFTA-DR textile exemption applies ONLY to textiles/apparel from the six CAFTA-DR countries (Costa Rica, Dominican Republic, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua)
- USMCA exemption applies to qualifying goods from Canada and Mexico, but this is a general USMCA exemption, not specific to textiles
- China is not covered by either CAFTA-DR or USMCA

The de minimis exemption was a pre-existing rule that allowed international shipments valued at under $800 to enter the US without paying tariffs. The de minimis provision was added to the Tariff Act of 1930 several years after the law passed and was intended to facilitate trade by eliminating the administrative burden of collecting modest import duties on low-cost goods.

In 2025, President Trump signed an Executive Order suspending duty-free de minimis tariff exemptions for low-value shipments from all countries. He first suspended de minimis treatment for low-value packages from China and Hong Kong on May 2, and then eliminated the de minimis exception for all countries except China after August 29, 2025.
